You are a Data Analyst at a music distributor. Your goal is to determine the revenue split between 'Full Album' purchases and 'Single/Partial' track purchases using the Chinook database (/home/ga/Documents/databases/chinook.db).

- Single/Partial Purchase: An invoice includes some, but not all, tracks from a specific AlbumId.

Generate a summary report with total and per-class counts at '~/Fiji_Data/results/colonies/colony_summary.txt' and a distribution table with columns for size_class, count, percentage, and mean_area at '~/Fiji_Data/results/colonies/size_distribution.csv'. Save an annotated image with colony overlays to '~/Fiji_Data/results/colonies/colony_overlay.png'.

You have access to Fiji with a histology sample image. Perform color deconvolution on the 'HeLa Cells (1.3M, 48-bit RGB)' sample image to separate the stain components. Save the first resulting channel (Colour_1) to ~/Fiji_Data/results/channel_1.png and the second resulting channel (Colour_2) to ~/Fiji_Data/results/channel_2.png. Record the measurement statistics for the first channel and save them to ~/Fiji_Data/results/channel_1_stats.csv.

Convert the Red/Green composite microscopy image at ~/Fiji_Data/raw/composite/unsafe_composite.tif into a colorblind-accessible Magenta/Green figure. Change Channel 1 to Magenta while keeping Channel 2 as Green. Save the resulting flattened RGB image to ~/Fiji_Data/results/figures/accessible_composite.png. Generate a Deuteranopia simulation of the figure and save it to ~/Fiji_Data/results/figures/deuteranopia_proof.png.

Quantify DNA damage in cells from the fluorescence microscopy image comet_sample.tif using the Comet Assay method. Analyze the three most prominent comets to determine the Integrated Density of the whole comet (head and tail) and the head alone. Calculate the percentage of tail DNA for each using the formula: % Tail DNA = (Whole Integrated Density - Head Integrated Density) / Whole Integrated Density * 100. Save the analysis results, including Comet ID, Whole Integrated Density, Head Integrated Density, and Percent Tail DNA, to ~/Fiji_Data/results/comet/comet_analysis.csv. Save a screenshot of the analyzed regions to ~/Fiji_Data/results/comet/roi_overlay.png.

You are a medical physicist calibrating a raw CT scan slice. Calibrate the 16-bit grayscale detector data in '~/Fiji_Data/raw/ct/patient_ct_raw.tif' to Hounsfield Units (HU) using Air (-1000 HU) and Water (0 HU) as reference points. Segment the skull bone tissue using a threshold of 400 HU to create a binary mask. Save the calibrated image as 'calibrated_ct.tif', the binary mask as 'bone_mask.png', and a density report as 'density_report.csv' (including Mean_HU for Air, Water, and Bone) to '~/Fiji_Data/results/ct/'. The calibrated image must preserve the full dynamic range.

Create a depth color-coded Z-projection of the 'FluorescentCells.tif' confocal microscopy stack to visualize 3D structure. Process the extracted Channel 2 (Green/Cytoskeleton) using background subtraction (rolling ball radius: 30 pixels) and contrast enhancement (0.3% saturated, normalized). Generate a standard Maximum Intensity Projection (MIP) saved to ~/Fiji_Data/results/depth_projection/standard_mip.png. Produce a depth color-coded RGB projection using the 'Spectrum' LUT with an added calibration bar, saving the result to ~/Fiji_Data/results/depth_projection/depth_coded_projection.png. Finally, save a text report to ~/Fiji_Data/results/depth_projection/stack_report.txt listing the original number of channels, number of Z-slices, and the LUT name used.

Perform quantitative DNA content analysis on a fluorescence microscopy image of human cancer cells located at ~/Fiji_Data/raw/cell_cycle/nuclei.tif. The analysis should include background subtraction (50-pixel rolling ball), nuclei segmentation using Otsu thresholding and watershed separation, and measurement of Integrated Density for particles with a size of 100-Infinity. Identify the G1 phase peak (mode) from the intensity distribution. Save the measurements table to ~/Fiji_Data/results/cell_cycle/measurements.csv, the histogram plot to ~/Fiji_Data/results/cell_cycle/histogram.png, and a text report containing the numeric G1 peak value to ~/Fiji_Data/results/cell_cycle/g1_peak_report.txt.

Measure the average lamellar spacing of the eutectic microstructure in 'AuPbSn40.tif' using a spatial calibration of 0.49 µm/pixel. Isolate the lamellar structure using a bandpass filter (filtering structures larger than 40 pixels and smaller than 3 pixels) and perform an FFT analysis to determine the average peak-to-peak spacing in microns. Save the FFT Power Spectrum to ~/Fiji_Data/results/fft_analysis/fft_power_spectrum.png, the bandpass filtered image to ~/Fiji_Data/results/fft_analysis/bandpass_filtered.png, the line profile data to ~/Fiji_Data/results/fft_analysis/line_profile.csv, and a text report containing the phrase 'lamellar spacing' and the measured value in µm to ~/Fiji_Data/results/fft_analysis/spacing_report.txt.

Correct the vignetting in the fluorescence microscopy image '~/Fiji_Data/raw/illumination_test/test_image.tif' using a pseudo-flat-field correction method (Gaussian blur sigma=50, 32-bit division). Export the corrected image to '~/Fiji_Data/results/flatfield/corrected_image.tif' and the flat-field reference to '~/Fiji_Data/results/flatfield/flatfield_reference.tif'. Document the mean intensity comparison of the center versus corners before and after correction in '~/Fiji_Data/results/flatfield/uniformity_report.csv' and provide a summary of the uniformity improvement in '~/Fiji_Data/results/flatfield/correction_summary.txt'.

You are a microscopy technician performing quality control on a batch of fluorescence images in `~/Fiji_Data/raw/BBBC005/`. Assess the focus quality of all 'w1' (nuclear stain) TIF images by calculating four metrics: Variance of Laplacian (VoL) (3x3 Laplacian kernel variance), Normalized Variance (Variance/Mean), Tenengrad (Mean of squared Sobel gradient magnitudes), and StdDev (Standard deviation of raw intensities). Rank the images by VoL from highest to lowest and classify each as 'PASS' if its VoL is greater than the batch median, or 'FAIL' otherwise. Generate a report CSV at `~/Fiji_Data/results/focus_qc/focus_metrics.csv` with columns: filename, laplacian_var, normalized_var, tenengrad, stddev, rank, and qc_status. Create a text summary at `~/Fiji_Data/results/focus_qc/qc_summary.txt` listing the total images, PASS/FAIL counts, best/worst focused filenames, and the median threshold used. Save a visual comparison montage of the best and worst focused images to `~/Fiji_Data/results/focus_qc/focus_comparison.png`.

Calculate the Porosity (Void Fraction) and Mean Pore Area of a sourdough bread slice using Fiji. Using the image at ~/Fiji_Data/raw/food_science/sourdough_slice.jpg (where the full width represents 14.0 cm), analyze a central 5x5 cm ROI to exclude the crust. Segment the dark air voids, filtering out particles with an area less than 0.005 cm². Save the resulting measurements to ~/Fiji_Data/results/food_science/pore_measurements.csv and a segmentation mask screenshot to ~/Fiji_Data/results/food_science/segmentation_check.png. Document the calculated Porosity % (Total Pore Area / ROI Area * 100) and Mean Pore Area in cm² in a report at ~/Fiji_Data/results/food_science/quality_report.txt.

Calculate the Fractal Dimension (D) of the objects in the 'blobs.gif' sample image using Fiji. Perform the analysis on the single-pixel outlines of the objects to determine the boundary fractal dimension. Save the resulting data table to ~/Fiji_Data/results/fractal/fractal_results.csv and the log-log box count plot to ~/Fiji_Data/results/fractal/fractal_plot.png.

Perform Sensitized Emission FRET correction on the fluorescence microscopy images 'donor_channel.tif', 'acceptor_channel.tif', and 'fret_raw_channel.tif' located in '~/Fiji_Data/raw/fret_experiment/'. Create a corrected image named 'corrected_fret.tif' using the formula: Corrected_FRET = Raw_FRET - (0.28 * Donor) - (0.19 * Acceptor). The calculation must be performed with 32-bit float precision to maintain accuracy. Save the resulting image to '~/Fiji_Data/results/fret/corrected_fret.tif'.

You are an astronomer analyzing star formation in the Whirlpool Galaxy (M51). Your task is to isolate and count young star clusters (HII regions) from the image at '~/Fiji_Data/raw/astronomy/m51.tif' by suppressing the diffuse galactic glow. Apply a Bandpass Filter (filtering large structures of 40 pixels and small structures of 3 pixels with autoscaling) and save the filtered image to '~/Fiji_Data/results/astronomy/m51_filtered.tif'. Segment the clusters using an automatic threshold and analyze particles within a size range of 5-500 pixels^2. Record the Area and Mean Gray Value for each cluster. Save the final measurements table to '~/Fiji_Data/results/astronomy/cluster_measurements.csv' and the analysis mask to '~/Fiji_Data/results/astronomy/cluster_mask.png'.

You are an astronomer analyzing the interacting galaxy pair M51 (Whirlpool Galaxy) and its companion NGC 5195. Segment the galaxies and separate the companion (NGC 5195) from the main spiral. Measure the Area, Centroid, Mean Gray Value, Integrated Density, and Fit Ellipse for the companion galaxy only. Save the measurements to ~/Fiji_Data/results/galaxy/companion_metrics.csv and save an image showing the segmentation mask or ROI outlines to ~/Fiji_Data/results/galaxy/segmentation_map.png.

You are a biochemist quantifying protein expression levels from a polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) image located at ~/Fiji_Data/raw/gel/protein_gel.tif. Quantify the band intensities for all lanes, applying background subtraction and using Lane 1 as the reference/loading control. Calculate the normalized intensity for each lane as a ratio relative to Lane 1. Save the quantification results including lane ID, raw intensity, normalized intensity, background intensity, and peak area percentage to ~/Fiji_Data/results/gel/band_quantification.csv. Save the lane intensity profile plots to ~/Fiji_Data/results/gel/lane_profiles.png. Generate a summary report at ~/Fiji_Data/results/gel/densitometry_report.txt that identifies the lanes with the highest and lowest expression relative to Lane 1 and lists all normalized values.

You are a materials scientist analyzing a eutectic alloy microstructure image (~/Fiji_Data/raw/alloy/AuPbSn40.jpg) to quantitatively discriminate between Phase A (brighter, finer-textured) and Phase B (darker, coarser-textured) using texture features. Define at least three square ROIs (~80-120 pixels per side) for each phase, naming them PhaseA_1, PhaseA_2, PhaseA_3 and PhaseB_1, PhaseB_2, PhaseB_3 respectively. Save the ROI set to ~/Fiji_Data/results/texture/rois.zip. Compute texture features (such as ASM, Contrast, Correlation, IDM, and Entropy via GLCM, or statistical measures from variance-filtered images) for each ROI. Save these measurements to ~/Fiji_Data/results/texture/glcm_measurements.csv, ensuring the file includes a header row and at least six rows of data. Generate an annotated overlay image showing the ROI locations on the microstructure and save it to ~/Fiji_Data/results/texture/annotated_microstructure.png. Write a summary report to ~/Fiji_Data/results/texture/texture_report.txt containing the Mean ± StdDev of each texture feature for both phases, an identification of the feature that best discriminates between them, and a conclusion on whether texture analysis successfully distinguishes the two phases.

You are a bioimage analyst. Reconstruct a 4D Hyperstack from raw TIFF files located at `~/Fiji_Data/raw/BBBC005/` for a specific field of view (Well: A01, Cell Density: C1, Site: s1). The raw data contains 2 channels (w1=Nuclear, w2=Cell Body) and multiple focal planes. The resulting hyperstack should be organized in xyCZT order with 2 channels and 1 timeframe. Save the reconstructed hyperstack to `~/Fiji_Data/results/reconstructed_hyperstack.tif`.

A materials scientist has two optical micrographs of the same polished alloy sample region. The second image (~/Fiji_Data/raw/registration/shifted_micrograph.tif) is misaligned relative to the reference (~/Fiji_Data/raw/registration/reference_micrograph.tif) due to translation and rotation. Register the shifted image to the reference and save the aligned result to ~/Fiji_Data/results/registration/registered_micrograph.tif. Create a difference image (Reference minus Registered) and save it to ~/Fiji_Data/results/registration/difference_image.tif. Record the estimated transformation parameters (Translation X, Translation Y, Rotation) and the registration method used in a text report at ~/Fiji_Data/results/registration/alignment_report.txt.

Calculate the Jaccard Index (Intersection over Union) for simulated microscopy images `channel_1.tif` (nuclear stain) and `channel_2.tif` (cytoplasmic stain) located at `~/Fiji_Data/raw/jaccard/` to quantify their spatial overlap. Use the Otsu auto-threshold method to create binary masks for both images. Produce an intersection mask and a union mask, saving them to `~/Fiji_Data/results/jaccard/intersection_mask.tif` and `~/Fiji_Data/results/jaccard/union_mask.tif` respectively. Record the calculated Jaccard Index, intersection area, and union area in `~/Fiji_Data/results/jaccard/results.csv` using the format: `jaccard_index,intersection_area,union_area`.

You are a medical scientist analyzing cancer cell migration in a time-lapse microscopy dataset of HeLa cells (nuclei labeled with H2B-GFP) in Fiji. Measure the migration velocity of at least three moving nuclei using kymograph analysis, ensuring the migration path used is at least 200 pixels long. Use a calibration of 0.645 µm per pixel and a time interval of 0.5 hours per frame. Calculate velocity using the formula: Velocity (µm/hr) = (dx_pixels * 0.645) / (dy_frames * 0.5). Save the generated kymograph to ~/Fiji_Data/results/kymograph/kymograph_main.png. Export a CSV file to ~/Fiji_Data/results/kymograph/velocity_measurements.csv with columns 'track_id' and 'velocity_um_per_hr' for at least three measurements. Save a summary report including the number of tracks and mean velocity to ~/Fiji_Data/results/kymograph/kymograph_report.txt.

You are a biologist studying leaf hydraulic architecture. Extract and analyze the vein network from the grayscale leaf image at ~/Fiji_Data/raw/leaf/leaf_grayscale.tif. Calibrate the image using a pixel size of 5.0 microns/pixel (unit: 'um'). Process the image to create a binary skeleton of the veins and perform a network topology analysis.

Save the following results to ~/Fiji_Data/results/skeleton/:
- leaf_skeleton.png: The final binary skeleton image.
- skeleton_overlay.png: An image showing the skeleton overlaid on the original leaf or a labeled skeleton map.
- network_topology.csv: A CSV file containing the skeleton analysis results, including columns for # Branches, # Junctions, and Maximum Branch Length.
- analysis_report.txt: A text summary stating the total number of branches, junctions, and the mean branch length in microns.

You are a materials scientist characterizing an Au-Pb-Sn solder alloy. Your goal is to measure the local thickness distribution of both the primary (bright) and matrix (dark) phases for the image at ~/Fiji_Data/raw/metallography/AuPbSn40_alloy.tif using the Local Thickness method. Generate thickness maps for the solid phase (solid_phase_thickness_map.tif) and the void/matrix phase (void_phase_thickness_map.tif). Record the mean, standard deviation, and maximum thickness for both phases in ~/Fiji_Data/results/thickness/thickness_statistics.csv using the columns: phase, mean_thickness_px, std_thickness_px, max_thickness_px. Finally, save a color-coded visualization of a thickness map as thickness_visualization.png and a histogram of the solid phase thickness as thickness_histogram.png in the results directory.

Record a Fiji macro named 'clean_and_segment.ijm' that automates a cleaning and segmentation workflow on the 'Blobs' sample image. The macro must perform background subtraction (rolling ball radius: 50 pixels), a median filter (radius: 2 pixels), and segmentation using the Otsu thresholding algorithm. Save the macro to ~/Fiji_Data/results/macros/clean_and_segment.ijm.

You are a metallurgist analyzing a sample of hot-rolled carbon steel to determine its mechanical properties. Quantify the percentage of the Pearlite phase (dark/textured regions) versus the Ferrite phase (light regions) in the microstructure image located at ~/Fiji_Data/raw/metallurgy/steel_microstructure.jpg. The analysis requires converting the image to 8-bit grayscale, applying a median filter with a 2.0-pixel radius, and segmenting the Pearlite phase using the Otsu auto-threshold method for dark regions. Measure the Area Fraction of the Pearlite phase. Save the binary segmentation mask to ~/Fiji_Data/results/metallurgy/pearlite_mask.png, the measurement results table to ~/Fiji_Data/results/metallurgy/phase_measurements.csv, and a text report to ~/Fiji_Data/results/metallurgy/report.txt containing exactly one line in the format: Pearlite_Fraction: XX.X.

Quantify mitochondrial network morphology in the fluorescence microscopy image located at ~/Fiji_Data/raw/mitochondria/mitochondria_raw.tif. Preprocess and segment the image to isolate the mitochondrial network. Calculate shape descriptors, specifically Aspect Ratio (AR) and Form Factor (Circularity), and save these measurements to ~/Fiji_Data/results/mitochondria/shape_metrics.csv. Perform a network topology analysis by skeletonizing the binary image and save the resulting skeleton metrics to ~/Fiji_Data/results/mitochondria/skeleton_metrics.csv. Additionally, save the labeled skeleton map visualization to ~/Fiji_Data/results/mitochondria/skeleton_map.png.

Quantify the fluorescence intensity of the left mitotic spindle pole over time in the 'Mitosis' sample image. Measure the Mean Gray Value of the left spindle pole (the bright spot on the left side of the dividing cell) in the Tubulin channel (channel 2) using a maximum intensity projection and a circular ROI (~15-20 px diameter) at frames 1, 10, 20, 30, and 40. Save the results to ~/Fiji_Data/results/tracking/spindle_intensity.csv with columns 'frame' and 'mean_intensity'.

You are a medical scientist analyzing a T1-weighted MRI brain scan. The dataset is located at ~/Fiji_Data/raw/t1-head/t1-head.tif. This is a 3D stack currently in axial orientation with no spatial calibration (units are pixels).

Your goal is to reformat this dataset, perform measurements, and generate a multi-view montage.

Required Steps:
1. Open the image (~/Fiji_Data/raw/t1-head/t1-head.tif).
2. Set Spatial Calibration (Image > Properties):
   - Pixel width: 1.0 mm
   - Pixel height: 1.0 mm
   - Voxel depth: 1.5 mm
   - Unit: mm
3. Navigate to a mid-axial slice (approx slice 65) showing the lateral ventricles.
4. Measure the maximum left-to-right brain width on this slice. Record the value.
5. Generate an Axial Line Profile:
   - Draw a line across the brain width.
   - Analyze > Plot Profile.
   - Save values as ~/Fiji_Data/results/reslice/axial_profile.csv
6. Reslice to Coronal View:
   - Select the original stack.
   - Image > Stacks > Reslice [/]... (Direction: Top).
   - Save the result as ~/Fiji_Data/results/reslice/coronal_stack.tif
7. Reslice to Sagittal View:
   - Select the original stack.
   - Image > Stacks > Reslice [/]... (Direction: Left).
   - Save the result as ~/Fiji_Data/results/reslice/sagittal_stack.tif
8. Generate a Coronal Line Profile:
   - On the new coronal stack, draw a vertical line from top to bottom through the brain.
   - Analyze > Plot Profile.
   - Save values as ~/Fiji_Data/results/reslice/coronal_profile.csv
9. Create an Orthogonal Montage:
   - Create a composite image showing one slice from each view (Axial, Coronal, Sagittal) side-by-side.
   - Save as ~/Fiji_Data/results/reslice/orthogonal_montage.png
10. Create a Measurement Report:
    - Create a text file at ~/Fiji_Data/results/reslice/measurement_report.txt
    - Include: The measured brain width (mm), dimensions of the coronal and sagittal stacks (slices, width, height), and the calibration values used.

Perform a quantitative analysis of spinal canal stenosis on the 'T1 Head' MRI dataset. Segment the spinal canal on slices 10 through 19 and measure the cross-sectional area for each slice. Export a CSV file containing 'Slice_ID' and 'Area' for these measurements to ~/Fiji_Data/results/stenosis/canal_measurements.csv. Identify the slice with the smallest area (maximum stenosis) and record the slice number in ~/Fiji_Data/results/stenosis/diagnosis.txt. Save a screenshot showing the segmentation of the narrowest slice to ~/Fiji_Data/results/stenosis/segmentation_evidence.png.

You are a physiologist analyzing a skeletal muscle biopsy to assess fiber size (atrophy/hypertrophy). Segment individual muscle fibers from the histological cross-section at ~/Fiji_Data/workspace/muscle_task/muscle_section.jpg and calculate their Mean Cross-Sectional Area (CSA). Use a scale of 1 pixel = 0.5 microns. When processing, ensure touching fibers are separated and filter the results to include only objects with an area between 500 um^2 and 20000 um^2. Save the detailed measurements to ~/Fiji_Data/results/muscle/results.csv, a visualization of the segmentation to ~/Fiji_Data/results/muscle/segmentation_check.png, and a text report containing the calculated Mean Fiber CSA in um^2 to ~/Fiji_Data/results/muscle/report.txt.

You are a materials scientist establishing a standard image preprocessing pipeline. Evaluate three noise reduction filters—Gaussian Blur (sigma=2.0), Median (radius=2), and Mean (radius=2)—on the noisy fluorescence microscopy image located at ~/Fiji_Data/raw/filter_test/test_image.tif.

Calculate the Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR = Mean / StdDev) for the original image and each filtered version. Save the processed images as gaussian_filtered.tif, median_filtered.tif, and mean_filtered.tif to ~/Fiji_Data/results/filter_comparison/.

Generate a comparison CSV named filter_comparison.csv in ~/Fiji_Data/results/filter_comparison/ with columns for filter, mean, stddev, min, max, and snr for the original and all filtered versions. Additionally, save a text report named filter_report.txt in the same folder that lists all SNR values and identifies the filter with the highest SNR as the "best" filter.

You are a pharmacologist performing a nuclear translocation assay to measure the Nuclear-to-Cytoplasmic (N/C) intensity ratio of a protein in a single cell. Using 'cell_dapi.tif' (nuclear marker) and 'cell_signal.tif' (protein signal) from ~/Fiji_Data/raw/translocation/, define a nuclear ROI and a cytoplasmic ROI consisting of a 15-pixel band surrounding the nucleus that excludes the nuclear area. Calculate the N/C ratio based on the Mean Gray Values of the protein signal within these two regions. Save the calculated ratio to ~/Fiji_Data/results/translocation/ratio_report.txt and export the ROIs to ~/Fiji_Data/results/translocation/rois.zip.

Perform automated particle tracking on the 5D microscopy hyperstack `~/Fiji_Data/raw/tracking/mitosis.tif` using the TrackMate plugin to track dividing nuclei/chromosomes. Use a Max Intensity Z-Projection of the chromosome channel for tracking. Configure the tracking pipeline using a LoG or DoG detector with an estimated blob diameter of ~8.0 microns and a threshold between 2.0-5.0. Use a Simple LAP tracker with a linking max distance of 10.0 microns, a gap-closing max distance of 10.0 microns, and a max frame gap of 2. Export the track statistics to `~/Fiji_Data/results/tracking/track_statistics.csv` and save a visual overlay of the tracks to `~/Fiji_Data/results/tracking/tracks_visual.png`.

Perform a quantitative inclusion analysis on the metallographic cross-section image located at ~/Fiji_Data/raw/particles/specimen_micrograph.tif. Calibrate the image using the scale of 0.1725 µm/pixel (referenced in ~/Fiji_Data/raw/particles/calibration_info.txt). Segment the dark inclusion particles, excluding any noise smaller than 5 µm². Measure the Area, Perimeter, Circularity, Solidity, Feret's Diameter, and Aspect Ratio for all valid particles and save the detailed results to ~/Fiji_Data/results/particles/particle_measurements.csv. Generate a size distribution histogram of particle areas and save it to ~/Fiji_Data/results/particles/size_distribution.png. Produce an annotated image showing particle outlines and a scale bar (10-50 µm) at ~/Fiji_Data/results/particles/annotated_micrograph.png. Finally, create a summary report at ~/Fiji_Data/results/particles/inclusion_report.txt including the total particle count, mean area (µm²), area fraction (%), the number of particles exceeding 500 µm², and a QC result (PASS if 5 or fewer particles exceed 500 µm², otherwise FAIL).

Calculate the confluence (percentage of area covered by cells) for the HeLa cell phase contrast image located at ~/Fiji_Data/raw/confluence/hela-cells.tif. Generate a binary mask where white pixels (255) represent cells and black pixels (0) represent background. Save the binary mask to ~/Fiji_Data/results/confluence/confluence_mask.png and record the measured confluence percentage as a single numeric value in ~/Fiji_Data/results/confluence/confluence_report.txt.

Characterize the optical resolution of the microscope system by measuring the Point Spread Function (PSF) using the bead image at ~/Fiji_Data/raw/psf_beads/bead_field.tif. Calibrate the image using a pixel size of 0.1625 µm/pixel. Determine the Full Width at Half Maximum (FWHM) for at least five isolated bead-like features. Record the results in ~/Fiji_Data/results/psf/fwhm_measurements.csv including columns for bead_id, fwhm_pixels, fwhm_um, and peak_intensity. Additionally, export a representative line profile plot to ~/Fiji_Data/results/psf/line_profile.png and a summary report containing the mean lateral resolution (mean FWHM in µm) to ~/Fiji_Data/results/psf/resolution_report.txt.

You are a molecular biologist preparing a figure for a publication. Create a comparative montage of 5 fluorescence microscopy images from ~/Fiji_Data/raw/BBBC005/ that share the same focus level (F1) but have different cell counts (C values). Standardize the display settings across all images using a global min/max brightness range and a Grays LUT to ensure matched contrast. Set the spatial scale for all images to 0.650 microns/pixel. Produce a labeled montage in a 1 row by 5 columns layout with a thin border, including a 50 micron scale bar in the lower right and labels 'Condition 1' through 'Condition 5' for the respective panels. Save the final montage as figure_montage.png and figure_montage.tif in ~/Fiji_Data/results/montage/. In the same folder, generate panel_metadata.csv (including panel_number, source_filename, display_min, display_max, and pixel_scale_um) and panel_statistics.csv (including panel_number, mean_intensity, and std_intensity). The final output must be publication-quality with matched contrast across all panels.

You are a microbiologist analyzing a colony plate image (blobs.gif) that suffers from uneven illumination (vignetting) where the corners are too dark for standard thresholding. Perform retrospective shading correction and segment all particles across the entire field of view, including the corners. Save the final binary mask as ~/Fiji_Data/results/corrected_mask.png and the measurement results, including the particle count, as ~/Fiji_Data/results/particle_count.csv.
Firefox is already open. Bookmark Wikipedia's main page (https://www.wikipedia.org) in the Bookmarks Toolbar or Bookmarks Menu folder.

You are a cybersecurity intelligence analyst. Build threat intelligence profiles for Sandworm (APT44/Voodoo Bear) and Lazarus Group (APT38/Hidden Cobra) using OSINT sources. Research each group's primary targets, at least 3 attack techniques, and known attribution. Organize research in Firefox bookmarks within a folder named 'APT Research' containing sub-folders 'Sandworm' and 'Lazarus Group' (minimum 3 source URLs per sub-folder). Save a consolidated report to ~/Desktop/threat_intel_report.txt including group names as headers, target sectors, 3 techniques with MITRE ATT&CK IDs per group, and the attributed nation-state sponsor.

You are a digital archivist. Curate a collection of 3 distinct 'Fireside Chat' audio recordings by President Franklin D. Roosevelt from the Internet Archive (archive.org). Download 3 different episodes as MP3 files (VBR or 64kbps, no zip files) to ~/Documents/FDR_Audio/, renaming them to the format 'fireside_chat_<topic>.mp3'. In the same directory, create a 'manifest.json' file containing a list of metadata objects for each recording, including 'filename', 'original_title', 'date', and 'source_url'.

Plan a stargazing session for the peak of the Geminids meteor shower in Tucson, Arizona on December 13, 2024. Using Firefox, find the local sunset time, astronomical twilight end time, moon illumination percentage, moon set time, Jupiter rise time, and Geminids meteor shower ZHR (Zenith Hourly Rate). Create a Firefox bookmark folder named 'Astronomy Tools' containing at least two bookmarks for the astronomical data sites used. Save the collected data to '~/Documents/stargazing_plan.json' using the keys: 'location', 'date', 'sunset', 'astronomical_twilight_end', 'moon_illumination_percent', 'moon_set_time', 'jupiter_rise_time', and 'geminids_zhr'.

Research three occupations on the Bureau of Labor Statistics Occupational Outlook Handbook (https://www.bls.gov/ooh/): Registered Nurses, Software Developers, and Electricians. Create a Firefox bookmark folder named 'Career Research' containing at least one BLS page for each occupation. Extract the Median Annual Wage, Job Outlook (%), Typical Entry-Level Education, and Number of Jobs for each. Save this data to ~/Documents/career_comparison.json using keys 'registered_nurses', 'software_developers', and 'electricians', with fields 'median_annual_wage' (integer), 'job_outlook_percent' (number), 'typical_education' (string), and 'num_jobs' (integer). You are a state epidemiologist preparing a weekly infectious disease briefing. Export the resulting heatmap as an 800x400 PNG image covering the world bounds (-180, -90, 180, 90) to /home/ga/heatmap_output.png.

Create a seamless Image Mosaic layer named 'global_mask' from the raster tiles located at 'file:mosaics/world_quadrants'. Using the GeoServer admin interface (http://localhost:8080/geoserver/web/, admin/Admin123!), configure an 'ImageMosaic' data store named 'world_mosaic_store' within a new workspace named 'imagery'. The final layer should be enabled with correctly computed bounding boxes to provide a single WMS layer combining the four quadrant images.

Create a thematic map style for world population in GeoServer (admin / Admin123!). Configure an SLD style named 'population_choropleth' in the 'ne' workspace with five classification rules for the 'pop_est' attribute: < 1,000,000 (Fill #ffffb2), 1,000,000 to 10,000,000 (Fill #fecc5c), 10,000,000 to 100,000,000 (Fill #fd8d3c), 100,000,000 to 500,000,000 (Fill #f03b20), and >= 500,000,000 (Fill #bd0026), all with a gray (#999999) stroke of width 0.5. Include labels for the 'name' property (SansSerif, size 10, color #333333, halo radius 2, color #ffffff). Set this as the default style for the 'ne:ne_countries' layer and export a WMS map image to /home/ga/output/population_map.png.

Create and apply an SLD style named 'country_semantic_zoom' to the 'ne:ne_countries' layer in the 'ne' workspace. The style must implement semantic zooming: render countries as polygons (fill #E0E0E0, stroke #333333) when the scale denominator is less than 35,000,000, and as centroid points (Circle, size 6, fill #FF0000) using a geometry transformation (centroid or interiorPoint) when the scale denominator is 35,000,000 or greater. Log in to GeoServer at http://localhost:8080/geoserver/web/ (User: admin, Password: Admin123!).

Create a new security configuration in GeoServer (http://localhost:8080/geoserver/web/) using credentials 'admin' and 'Admin123!'. Create a role named 'ROLE_DATA_ANALYST' and an enabled user named 'analyst_jones' with password 'Analyst2024!'. Assign the 'ROLE_DATA_ANALYST' role to 'analyst_jones'. Verify the new user's authentication against the GeoServer REST API and save the resulting HTTP status code to /home/ga/auth_test_result.txt.

Create a validated SLD 1.0.0 style named 'blue_polygon' in GeoServer (http://localhost:8080/geoserver/web/, credentials: admin / Admin123!). The style must render polygons with a solid blue fill (#0000FF) and a dark blue outline (#000080, 1 pixel width).

Create a WMS decoration layout named 'report_map' in the GeoServer data directory of the 'gs-app' container. The layout file at 'layouts/report_map.xml' must include a 'scaleline' decoration at the bottom-left (10px offset) and a 'text' decoration at the top-right (10px offset) with the message '© Natural Earth Data'. Generate and save an undecorated map image of 'ne:ne_countries' (EPSG:4326, 800x400) to '/home/ga/map_undecorated.png'. Additionally, generate a decorated map image using the 'report_map' layout with the same parameters and save it to '/home/ga/map_decorated.png'.

Create a new workspace in GeoServer named 'natural_earth' with the namespace URI 'http://naturalearthdata.com'. The GeoServer web admin interface is at http://localhost:8080/geoserver/web/ (credentials: admin / Admin123!).

Create and deploy a persistent WFS 2.0 Stored Query named 'GetLargeCities' on the GeoServer instance (http://localhost:8080/geoserver/web/, admin/Admin123!). The query must filter the 'ne:ne_populated_places' layer to return cities where the 'pop_max' attribute is greater than or equal to a provided integer parameter named 'minPop'. Save the XML response for a query with a minimum population of 10,000,000 to /home/ga/large_cities_response.xml.

A seismological institute has published a PostGIS-backed layer of real USGS earthquake events (M5.0+, January–March 2024) in GeoServer, but the time dimension has not been configured yet. The web mapping team needs time-enabled WMS so their JavaScript client can filter and animate earthquakes by date. Configure the WMS time dimension on the 'earthquakes' layer in the 'seismic' workspace using the 'event_time' attribute. Set the presentation mode to 'List' and the default value strategy to 'Use the biggest domain value'.

GeoServer admin credentials: admin / Admin123!
GeoServer URL: http://localhost:8080/geoserver/web/

Export the 'ne:ne_countries' layer from the GeoServer WFS service (http://localhost:8080/geoserver/wfs) for all countries where the 'continent' attribute is 'South America'. Generate the following four files in '~/exports/': 'south_america.geojson' (GeoJSON), 'south_america.kml' (KML), 'south_america.gml' (GML3), and 'south_america.csv' (CSV).

You are a GIS Platform Architect preparing for a high-traffic public map launch. Log in to GeoServer at http://localhost:8080/geoserver/web/ (admin / Admin123!). Configure tile caching for the ne:ne_countries layer to include the EPSG:900913 (Web Mercator) gridset, image/png format, and a metatiling factor of 4x4 or larger. Additionally, perform a tile seeding operation for the ne:ne_countries layer using the EPSG:4326 gridset and image/png format for zoom levels 0 to 3, covering the full world extent (minX=-180, minY=-90, maxX=180, maxY=90).

Configure a secure, multi-tenant environment for a new client 'Alpha Corp' in GeoServer (http://localhost:8080/geoserver/web/) using credentials 'admin' / 'Admin123!'. Establish an isolated workspace named 'alpha_corp' (URI: http://www.alphacorp.com/data) and a PostGIS store named 'alpha_ds' connecting to the 'gis' database (Host: gs-postgis, User: geoserver, Password: geoserver123). Publish the 'ne_rivers' table as a layer named 'secure_rivers' with the title 'Alpha Corp Secure Hydrography'. The configuration must ensure the layer is hidden from global WFS capabilities but remains accessible via workspace-specific WFS capabilities.

You are a GIS Lead at a regional planning authority. Configure a dual-workspace geospatial data portal in GeoServer (http://localhost:8080/geoserver/web/, admin / Admin123!). The portal must include an 'infrastructure' workspace (URI: http://infrastructure.authority.gov/gis) and an 'environment' workspace (URI: http://environment.authority.gov/gis), both connected to the PostGIS database at gs-postgis:5432/gis (geoserver/geoserver123) via datastores 'infra_data' and 'env_data' respectively. Publish 'ne_populated_places' as the 'settlements' layer in the infrastructure workspace and 'ne_rivers' as the 'waterways' layer in the environment workspace. Apply a custom SLD style 'settlement_marker' (orange #FFA500 circle, size 8) as the default for settlements and 'waterway_line' (blue #0080FF line, width 2) as the default for waterways. Both layers should be included in a global layer group named 'regional_portal'.

Create an SLD style named 'optimized_places' in the 'ne' workspace and set it as the default style for the 'ne:ne_populated_places' layer. The style must feature a size 6 red circle (#FF0000) symbol and black (#000000) Arial 12px bold labels using the 'name' attribute. Configure VendorOptions for 'autoWrap' at 80 pixels, 'maxDisplacement' at 50 pixels, and 'spaceAround' at 10 pixels. Export a map preview image of the layer focused on Europe to /home/ga/places_optimized.png.

Configure the 'ne:ne_countries' layer in GeoServer (http://localhost:8080/geoserver, admin / Admin123!) for production delivery. Enable HTTP Response Cache Headers with a cache time of 86400 seconds. Configure the WMS Attribution for the layer with the title 'Provided by Natural Earth', logo URL 'http://localhost:8080/geoserver/ne_logo.png', logo type 'image/png', width 88, and height 31.

A GIS technologist needs to serve Natural Earth country boundaries in Web Mercator projection (EPSG:3857) for a web mapping application. Create a new layer named 'ne_countries_3857' in the 'ne' workspace using the 'postgis_ne' data store and the 'ne_countries' PostGIS table. The layer must be reprojected from its native EPSG:4326 to a declared EPSG:3857. Set the title to 'Natural Earth Countries (Web Mercator)' and ensure bounding boxes are computed. The original 'ne_countries' layer must remain unchanged. GeoServer is at http://localhost:8080/geoserver/ with credentials admin / Admin123!.

Configure GeoServer (admin/Admin123!) to serve Natural Earth shapefiles currently located at /home/ga/natural_earth/. The shapefiles must be accessible to the GeoServer container at /opt/geoserver/data_dir/shp_data/. Establish a workspace named shp_ne (URI: http://shp_ne.example.org) and a directory-based store named shp_directory. Publish the layers ne_110m_admin_0_countries (Title: 'Countries (Shapefile)') and ne_110m_lakes (Title: 'Lakes (Shapefile)') with EPSG:4326 and calculated bounding boxes. Save WMS GetMap images for the countries and lakes layers to /home/ga/countries_map.png and /home/ga/lakes_map.png respectively.

Publish the 'ne_countries' table from the PostGIS database (host: 'gs-postgis', port: 5432, db: 'gis', user: 'geoserver', pass: 'geoserver123') as a layer in GeoServer (http://localhost:8080/geoserver/web/, credentials: admin/Admin123!). The layer must be published from a new PostGIS data store named 'postgis_natural_earth' within the 'cite' workspace and include computed bounding boxes.

Register a new custom Coordinate Reference System (CRS) in GeoServer (http://localhost:8080/geoserver/web/, User: admin, Password: Admin123!) with Code '990001' using the following WKT:

PROJCS["Pacific Mercator", 
  GEOGCS["WGS 84", 
    DATUM["World Geodetic System 1984", 
      SPHEROID["WGS 84", 6378137.0, 298.257223563]], 
    PRIMEM["Greenwich", 0.0], 
    UNIT["degree", 0.017453292519943295], 
    AXIS["Geodetic longitude", EAST], 
    AXIS["Geodetic latitude", NORTH]], 
  PROJECTION["Mercator_1SP"], 
  PARAMETER["central_meridian", 150.0], 
  PARAMETER["latitude_of_origin", 0.0], 
  PARAMETER["scale_factor", 1.0], 
  PARAMETER["false_easting", 0.0], 
  PARAMETER["false_northing", 0.0], 
  UNIT["m", 1.0], 
  AXIS["Easting", EAST], 
  AXIS["Northing", NORTH], 
  AUTHORITY["EPSG","990001"]]

Publish a new layer named 'ne_countries_pacific' in the 'ne' workspace using the 'ne_countries' table from the 'postgis_ne' store. The layer must be configured to use 'EPSG:990001' as the Declared SRS with SRS Handling set to 'Reproject native to declared' and have its bounding boxes computed.

Configure the 'ne_countries' layer in the 'ne' workspace to only advertise and accept requests in 'EPSG:4326' and 'EPSG:3857' using the GeoServer Web Administration Interface (http://localhost:8080/geoserver/web/, user: admin, pass: Admin123!). Ensure the layer's WMS settings are restricted to these two SRS codes and the response policy excludes all others.

Configure GeoServer Service Security at http://localhost:8080/geoserver/web/ (Username: 'admin', Password: 'Admin123!') to restrict data modification capabilities. Lock down the WFS 'Transaction' operation so only the 'ADMIN' role can use it, while keeping WFS 'GetFeature' (read access) open to the 'Anonymous' role.

You are a calculus professor creating 3D course materials for a Calculus II unit on solids of revolution. In GeoGebra, create an interactive visualization demonstrating the solid formed by rotating the curve f(x) = sqrt(x) over the interval [0, 4] around the x-axis. The visualization must include the original curve, the surface of revolution, a slider controlling a moveable cross-sectional plane at position x = a showing the circular cross-section at that x-value, and a text display showing the computed volume from 0 to the slider value a using the disk method formula. Save the file as 'solid_revolution.ggb' in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/

Create a construction plan for an analemmatic sundial in Chicago (Latitude 41.88° N) with a semi-major axis of 6 units. The construction must include an ellipse for the hour markers with a semi-minor axis calculated as b = a * sin(lat), and points for the hour markers at 12:00 (Noon at (0, b)), 9:00 AM, and 3:00 PM (at (a * sin(45°), b * cos(45°))). Additionally, mark the Summer and Winter Solstice on the Y-axis date strip using the formula y = a * tan(23.44°) * cos(lat). Include text labels for 'Noon', 'Summer', and 'Winter' and save the file as ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/sundial_chicago.ggb.

Create an interactive GeoGebra applet demonstrating Archimedes' method of exhaustion for approximating π. The construction must include a unit circle centered at the origin (0, 0) with radius 1 and an integer slider named 'n' (ranging from 3 to at least 48) that dynamically controls the number of sides for both a regular inscribed n-gon and a regular circumscribed n-gon. Display the numerical lower bound (inscribed polygon's half-perimeter) and upper bound (circumscribed polygon's half-perimeter) for π, along with a text annotation on the Graphics view referencing π and the computed approximation values. Save the completed construction as ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/archimedes_pi.ggb.

You are an acoustics engineer designing a subwoofer array layout. Visualize the 'cancellation alleys' (destructive interference zones) for a dual-subwoofer setup operating at a test frequency of 170 Hz (speed of sound: 340 m/s). With subwoofers located at (-2, 0) and (2, 0), construct the nodal lines representing destructive interference and identify the 'dead spots' where these lines intersect the audience row at y = 5. Save your work as 'subwoofer_array.ggb' in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/.

Create a physics simulation in GeoGebra comparing the trajectory of a baseball in a vacuum versus in air with drag (Fd = 0.5 * ρ * Cd * A * v²). Use the following parameters: Mass m = 0.145 kg, Drag Coefficient Cd = 0.30, Cross-sectional Area A = 0.0043 m², Air Density ρ = 1.225 kg/m², Initial Velocity v0 = 44.7 m/s, Launch Angle θ = 30°, and Gravity g = 9.8 m/s². The simulation must model both trajectories and identify the landing points (y=0) for each. Save the file as baseball_physics.ggb in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/.

Create a physics-based simulation of a basketball bank shot in GeoGebra to visualize the trajectory of a ball reflecting off a backboard. The simulation should use gravity g = 9.8 m/s², a release height of (0, 2) meters, a vertical backboard at x = 4.6 meters, and a hoop at y = 3.05 meters (rim center at x = 4.15). Include sliders for initial velocity (v0, range 5 to 15 m/s), angle (theta, range 30° to 85°), and coefficient of restitution (e, range 0 to 1). The trajectory must model standard projectile motion until it hits the backboard, followed by an elastic collision rebound where the horizontal velocity reverses and scales by the coefficient of restitution (vx_new = -e * vx_old) while vertical motion continues under gravity. Visualize the flight path and the backboard segment. Save the simulation to ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/bank_shot.ggb.

You are a mechanical engineer designing a flat belt drive system. Model a two-pulley system in GeoGebra consisting of a Driver Pulley (Radius = 50 mm, center (0, 0)) and a Driven Pulley (Radius = 125 mm, center (500, 0)) connected by an open belt path using external common tangents. Construct the geometry, measure the Wrap Angle on the smaller Driver pulley, and calculate the Total Belt Length (sum of the straight segments and contact arcs). Display these values on the construction and save the design as 'belt_drive.ggb' in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/.

Create an interactive GeoGebra applet demonstrating the de Casteljau algorithm for a cubic Bézier curve using control points P0(0,0), P1(1,3), P2(4,3), and P3(5,0). The applet must include a slider 't' (0 to 1), the control polygon segments, the intermediate de Casteljau points that move with 't', the final point on the curve, and the full cubic Bézier curve trace. Save the project as 'bezier_decasteljau.ggb' in '~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/'.

You are a bicycle frame builder verifying the geometry of a new Enduro mountain bike design. Construct the front-end geometry in GeoGebra and measure the 'Ground Trail' based on a 29-inch wheel (Radius = 370 mm), a 67-degree head tube angle (from horizontal ground), and a 44 mm fork rake (offset). The bike should be oriented facing LEFT with the wheel ahead of the steering axis. The construction must include the ground line, the wheel tangent to the ground, and the steering axis. Measure the distance between the wheel's contact point and the steering axis intercept on the ground. Add a text annotation displaying the result (e.g., 'Trail = [value] mm') and save the project as 'bicycle_trail.ggb' in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/.

Create a biomechanical gait animation using motion capture data from '~/Documents/GeoGebra/data/gait_kinematics.csv'. Construct a 2-segment leg model with a thigh and shank (each 4 units long) starting from a fixed Hip point (H) at (0, 5). The positions of the Knee (K) and Ankle (A) must be driven by the 'HipAngle' and 'KneeAngle' data columns from the file, synchronized by an integer slider 'n' (range 1 to 51). Connect the joints with segments H-K and K-A. Save the final project as 'gait_viz.ggb' in '~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/'.

You are a calculus professor preparing interactive course materials for a Calculus I unit on derivatives and critical points. Create a GeoGebra visualization for the function f(x) = x^3 - 3x + 1. The visualization must include the function f(x) and its derivative f'(x) plotted together, an interactive tangent line that moves along the curve, identified critical points where f'(x) = 0, and a display of numerical values showing the instantaneous rate of change at the moveable point. Save the file as 'derivative_explorer.ggb' in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/

Design a radial plate cam profile for an automated packaging machine. The cam must rotate 360 degrees with a base circle radius of 3 units and drive a follower with the following displacement sequence: (1) 0°-90°: Dwell at 0 lift; (2) 90°-180°: Rise 2 units using Simple Harmonic Motion; (3) 180°-270°: Dwell at 2 units lift; (4) 270°-360°: Return to 0 units using Simple Harmonic Motion. Create a parametric curve in GeoGebra that represents this cam profile and save the project as 'cam_profile.ggb' in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/.

Create a GeoGebra visualization of the 'Chaos Game' fractal (Sierpinski Triangle) using London (-0.1, 51.5), Paris (2.3, 48.9), and Brussels (4.3, 50.8) as vertices. Generate a fractal point cloud of at least 500 iterations using the Chaos Game algorithm where each next point is the midpoint between the current point and a randomly selected vertex. Save the project as 'chaos_europe.ggb' in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/.

Create an interactive GeoGebra applet to demonstrate circle inversion. Construct a circle centered at the origin (0,0) with radius 3 and a free point P. Construct the inverse of point P with respect to the circle. Create a line that does not pass through the origin (e.g., x = 4) and construct its inverse with respect to the circle. Include a text annotation showing the product of distances OP * OP'. Save the project as 'circle_inversion.ggb' in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/.

Analyze the Mauna Loa CO2 record from ~/Documents/GeoGebra/data/co2_mlo_monthly.csv to separate the long-term trend from the seasonal cycle and forecast future levels. Use 'decimal date' (Column C) for the x-axis and 'average' (Column E) for the y-axis. Create a model Trend(x) for the long-term rise and a model Cycle(x) for the annual oscillation, then combine them into a single Model(x). Create a point named 'Prediction' that forecasts the CO2 level for May 2030 (x ≈ 2030.375) using your combined model. Save the project as ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/co2_analysis.ggb.

You are a forensic engineer analyzing a roller coaster's dynamics. Using the reference photo at '~/Documents/GeoGebra/assets/coaster_loop.jpg', reconstruct the loop's geometry in GeoGebra as a clothoid (Euler spiral) using Fresnel integrals (cos(t²) and sin(t²)). Calibrate the scale based on a track gauge of 1.2 meters and fit the curve to the track center line. Identify the loop's height in meters with a point and label at the top. Save the project as 'coaster_reconstruction.ggb' in '~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/'.

You are a mathematics tutor preparing a GeoGebra demonstration for a college student learning about complex numbers and roots of unity. Create an interactive visualization showing the five 5th roots of unity (solutions to z^5 = 1). The construction should feature a unit circle centered at the origin, five points representing the roots at angles 0°, 72°, 144°, 216°, and 288°, and a polygon connecting these points to form a regular pentagon. Include an angle slider (0° to 360°) and at least one text annotation such as '5th Roots of Unity' or 'z^5 = 1'. Save the completed construction to ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/roots_of_unity.ggb.

You are a Precalculus teacher creating an interactive GeoGebra applet that demonstrates how all conic sections (circle, ellipse, parabola, hyperbola) are one unified curve family controlled by a single parameter: eccentricity. Create a construction in GeoGebra featuring an eccentricity slider named 'e' (range 0 to 2, increment 0.01), a fixed semi-latus rectum value l = 2, and a focus point F at the origin (0, 0). Include a parametric conic curve defined by the polar focus-directrix equation r = l / (1 + e * cos(θ)), implemented using the Cartesian parametric form: Curve[ (l/(1 + e*cos(t))) * cos(t), (l/(1 + e*cos(t))) * sin(t), t, 0, 6.28 ]. Add at least one text annotation on the canvas describing the conic type or the eccentricity value. Save the construction as 'conic_eccentricity.ggb' in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/

Construct an equilateral triangle in GeoGebra where all three sides are equal and all three angles are 60 degrees (within ±2° tolerance). You may use any method to determine the vertex positions, but you must use the Polygon tool to create the final triangle for verification. Save the construction as 'equilateral_triangle.ggb' in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/

Model the Drax Power Station cooling tower in GeoGebra using the profile points: Base (0, 46), Throat (85, 26), and Top (114, 27.5). The model must feature a polynomial function passing through these points and a 3D surface of revolution generated by rotating the function around the x-axis for 0 ≤ x ≤ 114. Calculate the internal volume and include a text annotation showing the capacity in Olympic swimming pools (1 pool = 2500 m³) formatted as 'Capacity: [number] pools'. Save the project to ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/cooling_tower.ggb.

Create a 3D geometric visualization of the cross product of vectors u = (1, 2, -2) and v = (3, 0, 1) in GeoGebra. The visualization must include the cross product vector, the parallelogram spanned by vectors u and v, and a text annotation showing the result or the magnitude/area. Save the project as cross_product_3d.ggb in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/.

Create a 3D construction in GeoGebra demonstrating the hexagonal cross-section of a cube. Construct a cube of side length 2 with one vertex at the origin (0,0,0) and the opposite vertex at (2,2,2). Create a plane that passes through the center of the cube (1,1,1) and is perpendicular to the space diagonal connecting (0,0,0) and (2,2,2). Generate the intersection polygon representing the cross-section and display its area using a text annotation. Save the project as 'cube_hexagon.ggb' in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/

Create an animated GeoGebra construction demonstrating a cycloid—the curve traced by a point on the rim of a rolling circle. The construction must include a numeric slider 't' ranging from 0 to 12.5, a circle of radius 1 with its center at (t, 1), and a point on the rim at (t - sin(t), 1 - cos(t)) that traces the cycloid. Include the full cycloid path for the range 0 ≤ t ≤ 4π and a text annotation labeling the curve. Save the project as 'cycloid_animation.ggb' in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/.

Create an interactive microeconomics simulation of the Consumer Choice Problem in GeoGebra. The scenario involves a consumer with a Cobb-Douglas utility function U(x,y) = x*y facing a budget constraint I = x*Px + y*Py. The worksheet must include dynamic sliders for Income (I) with a range of 0 to 100, and prices Px and Py with ranges of 1 to 20. The simulation must visualize the Budget Line in red, the Optimal Consumption Bundle as a point labeled 'Optimal', and the Indifference Curve passing through the optimal bundle in blue. Save the file as 'utility_max.ggb' in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/.

You are a mathematics teacher preparing a visual proof that a ray from one focus reflects off the ellipse toward the other focus. Create an interactive GeoGebra demonstration of the reflective property of ellipses using an ellipse with semi-major axis a=5 and semi-minor axis b=4 (x²/25 + y²/16 = 1) and foci F1(-3, 0) and F2(3, 0). The demonstration must include a moveable point P constrained to the ellipse, the tangent line at P, and segments F1P and PF2. Measure the angles between the tangent line and the segments to demonstrate the law of reflection and include a text annotation 'Reflection Property'. Save the project as 'elliptic_billiard.ggb' in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/.

You are a public health researcher analyzing a 1978 influenza outbreak in a boarding school. Using the case data in '~/Documents/GeoGebra/data/flu_1978.csv', calibrate a dynamic SIR model (S' = -beta * S * I / N, I' = beta * S * I / N - gamma * I, R' = gamma * I) where N = 763, S(0)=762, I(0)=1, and R(0)=0. Fit the model's infected curve to the observed data for the period t=0 to t=15 by determining appropriate values for the transmission rate (beta) and recovery rate (gamma). Ensure the infected curve is visually distinct from the data points. Save the project to '~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/sir_analysis.ggb'.

Construct the Euler line for triangle ABC with vertices A(1, 1), B(7, 2), and C(4, 6) by geometrically constructing the Centroid (G), Circumcenter (O), and Orthocenter (H). Include a text annotation identifying the centers or describing the line. Save the project to ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/euler_line.ggb.

Create an interactive GeoGebra applet demonstrating Fourier series convergence to a square wave with period 2π and amplitude 1. The applet must include an integer slider n ranging from 1 to 20 and the Fourier partial sum function g(x) = (4/π) * Σ [sin((2k-1)x)/(2k-1)] for k from 1 to n. Style the functions distinctly, include a text annotation displaying the value of n, and save the project as fourier_square_wave.ggb in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/.

Create a GeoGebra file comparing two mathematical models of the St. Louis Gateway Arch: an inverted catenary f(x) = 630 - 128*(cosh(x/128) - 1) and a parabolic approximation g(x) = 630*(1 - (x/315)^2). Ensure both curves are visible in the Graphics View and include a text annotation labeling or comparing the curves. Save the project as 'gateway_arch_comparison.ggb' in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/.

Graph the quadratic function f(x) = x² - 4x + 3. Identify and mark at least three key points on the resulting parabola, such as the vertex, x-intercepts, or y-intercept. Save the construction to ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/quadratic_graph.ggb.

Import the Hurricane Katrina track data from ~/Documents/GeoGebra/data/katrina_track.csv and visualize the storm's path. Create a polyline connecting the track points (mapping Longitude to X and Latitude to Y) in chronological order. Calculate the total distance of the track in kilometers, assuming 1 degree equals 111.32 km, and display this value on the screen using a text label formatted as 'Total Distance: [value] km'. Save the project as 'katrina_analysis.ggb' in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/.

Create an interactive geometry demonstration of the Inscribed Angle Theorem in GeoGebra. Construct a circle centered at the origin (0, 0) with radius 3, featuring fixed points A at (3, 0) and B at (0, 3), and a moveable point C constrained to the circle's major arc. The demonstration must measure and display the inscribed angle ∠ACB and the central angle ∠AOB, and include a text annotation showing the relationship between these angles (e.g., "Central Angle = 2 * Inscribed Angle"). Save the completed construction as 'inscribed_angle.ggb' in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/.

Create a statics diagram of an L-shaped beam with vertices (0,0), (6,0), (6,2), (2,2), (2,8), and (0,8) to determine its Center of Mass and equilibrium suspension angle. Identify the global Centroid G and construct a plumb line passing through the Suspension Point S (0,8) and G. Measure the angle between the vertical edge (x=0) and this suspension line. Save the project as 'l_shape_statics.ggb' in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/.

Create an interactive GeoGebra worksheet that demonstrates the 'Least Squares' regression concept using points A=(1, 2), B=(2, 3), C=(3, 5), D=(4, 4), and E=(5, 7). The worksheet must feature a moveable 'guess' line, vertical residuals from each point to the line, and geometric squares on those residuals to visualize squared errors. Calculate and display the sum of the areas of these squares (the Sum of Squared Residuals) and include the actual regression line for comparison. Save the project as 'least_squares.ggb' in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/.

You are a university linear algebra instructor preparing a GeoGebra demonstration for your lecture on eigenvalues and eigenvectors. Create a construction in GeoGebra that visualizes the linear transformation defined by the matrix A = {{2,1},{1,2}} (from Strang's Introduction to Linear Algebra, Example 6.1). The construction must include a unit square with vertices at (0,0), (1,0), (1,1), and (0,1), and its transformed image under matrix A. Include vectors from the origin representing the eigenvectors for each eigenvalue of A, and a text annotation displaying the eigenvalues and the determinant of the matrix. Save the construction as 'linear_transform_eigen.ggb' in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/.

Create an interactive Lissajous figure explorer in GeoGebra for parametric curves defined by x(t) = sin(a · t + δ) and y(t) = sin(b · t) where t ranges from 0 to 2π. The construction must include a slider 'a' for x-frequency (1 to 5, increment 1), a slider 'b' for y-frequency (1 to 5, increment 1), and a slider 'd' for phase difference δ (0 to 2π, increment 0.01). Include the parametric curve and at least one text annotation showing the parametric equation, the frequency ratio, or a description. Save the construction as ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/lissajous_explorer.ggb.

Create an interactive GeoGebra applet to visualize the logistic growth differential equation dy/dx = r * y * (1 - y/K). The applet must include a slider for the growth rate 'r' (0.1 to 2, increment 0.1) and a slider for the carrying capacity 'K' (1 to 20, increment 1). Visualize the vector field for this ODE and plot at least three solution curves with starting values y(0)=1, y(0)=5, and y(0)=15. Include a text annotation explaining the equilibrium behavior at y=K. Save the project as 'logistic_slopefield.ggb' in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/.

You are a logistics planner visualizing service regions for London airports. Create a Voronoi Diagram representing the catchment areas for the six major London airports based on their coordinates relative to Central London (0,0): Heathrow (-26, 3), Gatwick (-2, -42), Stansted (50, 52), Luton (-36, 53), London City (11, 3), and Southend (63, 6). The diagram must be generated from a list of these points and include a text label 'Service Areas'. Save the project to ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/london_airports_voronoi.ggb

Create a Lorenz Curve visualization in GeoGebra using 2020 US Census data. Use the following income shares by quintile: Lowest Fifth (3.0%), Second Fifth (8.1%), Third Fifth (14.0%), Fourth Fifth (22.6%), and Highest Fifth (52.3%). The visualization must include the Lorenz Curve fitted to the cumulative income shares, the Line of Perfect Equality (y = x), and a text annotation showing the calculated Gini Coefficient. Save the project as 'lorenz_inequality.ggb' in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/.

You are a secondary school Business Math teacher creating an interactive GeoGebra applet to demonstrate the graphical method for solving a 2-variable linear programming problem.

Create a visualization of the following crop planning optimization problem:

A family farm has 240 acres for planting corn (x acres) and soybeans (y acres). Corn yields $250/acre net profit; soybeans yield $200/acre.

Maximize: P = 250x + 200y

Subject to:
1. Land: x + y ≤ 240
2. Water: 2x + y ≤ 400
3. Labor: x + 3y ≤ 480
4. x ≥ 0, y ≥ 0

Your GeoGebra file must contain:
1. The three main constraint boundaries graphed (as inequalities or lines)
2. At least 3 corner points of the feasible region marked as named points
3. The feasible region visually indicated (via inequality shading, a polygon, or similar)
4. At least one text annotation showing the optimal solution and/or maximum profit value

Save the file as 'crop_optimization.ggb' in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/

Create a scale model of the orbits of Mars and Earth in GeoGebra using Kepler's First Law and NASA/JPL data. Position the Sun at the origin (0,0). Model Earth's orbit as a circle with radius 1 AU centered at the Sun. Model Mars's orbit as an ellipse with the Sun at one focus, using a semi-major axis (a) of 1.524 AU and eccentricity (e) of 0.0934. The model must include labeled 'Perihelion' and 'Aphelion' points on Mars's orbit and a text label displaying the eccentricity value (e = 0.0934). Save the project as 'mars_orbit.ggb' to ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/.

Create a dynamic Mohr's Circle applet for stress analysis. The applet must include three sliders: 'sig_x' (range -100 to 100), 'sig_y' (range -100 to 100), and 'tau' (range 0 to 100). Construct the stress state using Point A (sig_x, -tau) and Point B (sig_y, tau), with a circle defined by diameter AB. Identify the Principal Stresses as the intersection points of the circle with the x-axis. Set the sliders to the calibration values sig_x = 80, sig_y = -40, and tau = 25, then save the project as 'mohrs_circle.ggb' in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/.

Create a GeoGebra demonstration of Napoleon's Theorem starting with a scalene triangle ABC with vertices A(0, 0), B(6, 0), and C(2, 5). Construct equilateral triangles external to each side of ABC and identify their centroids. Form the 'Napoleon Triangle' by connecting these centroids and include a dynamic measurement or text annotation verifying that the Napoleon Triangle is equilateral. Save the project as 'napoleon_theorem.ggb' in '~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/'.

Create a visualization of Newton's Method for finding roots for the function f(x) = x^3 - 2x - 5 (Newton's original example) starting at x0 = 3. The visualization should demonstrate at least 3 iterations, mark the approximate root on the x-axis, and include a text annotation displaying the final approximate root value. Save the project as 'newtons_method.ggb' in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/.

Create an interactive GeoGebra visualization of the Normal Distribution for US adult male heights (Mean μ = 175.4 cm, Standard Deviation σ = 7.2 cm) illustrating the Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7). The visualization must include the Normal PDF function, shaded regions under the curve representing ±1σ, ±2σ, and ±3σ, and text annotations for '68%', '95%', and '99.7%'. Save the file as 'normal_empirical_rule.ggb' in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/.

Create an interactive physics simulation of light refraction in GeoGebra that demonstrates Snell's Law (n1 * sin(theta1) = n2 * sin(theta2)). The construction must feature two sliders named 'n1' and 'n2' (range 1.0 to 2.5) representing refractive indices, a light source point, and an incident ray approaching a horizontal interface at the x-axis. Ensure the refracted ray updates dynamically and correctly handles Total Internal Reflection (TIR) by disappearing or becoming undefined when the critical angle is exceeded. Save the file as 'refraction_lab.ggb' in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/.

You are a pre-calculus teacher creating an interactive GeoGebra lesson on conic sections. Create a construction demonstrating the geometric definition of a parabola as the locus of points equidistant from a focus F at (0, 1) and a directrix line y = -1. The parabola must be generated geometrically as a locus rather than by entering a function formula. The construction must include the focus, the directrix, a moveable point on the directrix, the locus trace, and an annotation demonstrating the equal-distance property at a point on the parabola. Save the construction as 'parabola_locus.ggb' in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/

Design a 3D bottle shape using GeoGebra that meets specific engineering constraints. Create a rotationally symmetric bottle with a height of exactly 15 cm and an internal volume of 500 ml (± 5 ml). The project must include a profile function r(x) defining the radius along the height (x-axis from 0 to 15), a 3D visualization of the surface of revolution, and a dynamic volume calculation based on the disk method integral. Save the project as 'bottle_design.ggb' in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/. Note: 1 ml = 1 cubic cm; units in GeoGebra are treated as cm.

Create a geometrically correct 4x4 checkerboard grid in 2-point perspective in GeoGebra using a horizon line at y=4 and two vanishing points. The grid must feature mathematically accurate depth spacing rather than estimated positions. Save the file to ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/perspective_grid.ggb.

You are a clinical pharmacologist analyzing the therapeutic window of a new oral drug. Using GeoGebra, model the concentration-time profile for a single oral dose using the Bateman equation: C(t) = (D * ka / (V * (ka - ke))) * (e^(-ke * t) - e^(-ka * t)). Use the parameters: Dose (D) = 500 mg, Volume of distribution (V) = 40 L, Absorption rate (ka) = 1.2 / hr, and Elimination rate (ke) = 0.25 / hr. The model must include the therapeutic window boundaries at 5.0 mg/L and 12.0 mg/L. Calculate the 'Time in Therapeutic Range' (duration) where the concentration is above the minimum effective level and save the project as 'pk_analysis.ggb' to ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/.

You are an astrophysics student analyzing the Pleiades star cluster using data from '~/Documents/GeoGebra/data/pleiades_raw_data.csv'. Create a Hertzsprung-Russell (HR) Diagram in GeoGebra by calculating the Absolute Magnitude (M) for each star using a Distance Modulus of 5.6 (M = m - 5.6). Plot the stars with B-V Color on the X-axis and negative Absolute Magnitude (-M) on the Y-axis. Draw a polygon enclosing the main band of stars and add a text label "Main Sequence" near the polygon. Save the project as 'pleiades_analysis.ggb' in '~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/'.

You are a tutor preparing an interactive polar curve gallery in GeoGebra for a Calculus II student who struggles to visualize polar equations. Create a construction that includes a three-petal rose ($r = \sin(3\theta)$), a cardioid ($r = 1 + \cos(\theta)$), and a parameterized rose ($r = \sin(n\theta)$) controlled by a slider named 'n' with a range of 1 to 8 and an increment of 1. Add text annotations identifying the curves, such as "Three-petal rose: r = sin(3θ)" and "Cardioid: r = 1 + cos(θ)". Save the construction as polar_curves.ggb in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/.

Create a statistical analysis worksheet in GeoGebra using the inauguration ages of all 46 U.S. Presidents: {57, 61, 57, 57, 58, 57, 61, 54, 68, 51, 49, 64, 50, 48, 65, 52, 56, 46, 54, 49, 51, 47, 55, 55, 54, 42, 51, 56, 55, 51, 54, 51, 60, 62, 43, 55, 56, 61, 52, 69, 64, 46, 54, 47, 70, 78}. You are an AP Statistics teacher preparing a data analysis lesson on the relationship between national income and population health outcomes. Construct a Reuleaux triangle in GeoGebra using vertices A = (0, 0), B = (4, 0), and C = (2, 2*sqrt(3)). Create an interactive Riemann sum exploration in GeoGebra for the function f(x) = sin(x) on the interval [0, π]. Create a 2D kinematic simulation of an ABB IRB 120 robot arm in GeoGebra with Link 1 (290 mm) and Link 2 (302 mm).
